---
title: "Big Data, Big Experiments, and Big Problems"
author: "Susan Vanderplas"
date: "Jan 12, 2020"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    self_contained: true
    mathjax: "https://cdn.bootcss.com/mathjax/2.7.1/MathJax.js?config=TeX-MML-AM_HTMLorMML"
    css: ["default", "css/csafe.css", "css/csafe-fonts.css", "css/this-presentation.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      countIncrementalSlides: false
---
class: primary
```{r setup, echo = F, cache = F, include = F}
library(magrittr)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
mytheme <- theme_bw() + 
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey50"),
        panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey60"))

theme_set(mytheme)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, message = F, warning = F, cache = T, dpi = 300, dev = 'png')

```

class:primary,middle,center
## Slides at: 
<h3>srvanderplas.github.io/Presentations/PAG2020/</h3>

---
class:inverse
# Alternate Title: <br/>10 ways to screw up your experiment

---
class:primary
## Disclaimer

.large[All of these examples are real. 

*Most* of the mistakes I discuss are my own or occurred on projects I was a major participant in.
]

--

.large[.move-down[
***
Everyone screws up. My goal is to ensure that we find new ways to screw up. 
]]

<div class="move-margin" style="padding-top:80px">
<img src="https://media.giphy.com/media/emCxAd8L3cEwM/giphy.gif" alt="tiger yawning"/>
<p class="small">Making the same mistakes is boring</p>
</div>

---
class:inverse
# \# 1. Jump Right In: Start a GIANT study in a new field

---
class:primary
## Jump Right In: <br/>Longitudinal Shoe Study

- 160 Pairs of shoes, each worn for ~6 months

- 2 shoe models with different patterns and sole materials

- 7 different methods for imaging the shoes

- 3-4 different timepoints collected for each pair of shoes

<table class="blank" width="100%">
<tr><td><img src="small-005772L_20171031_2_1_1_csafe_bpbryson.jpg" alt="Digital Scan Image - Nike"/></td><td><img src="small-005772L_20171213_4_1_1_csafe_jekruse.jpg" alt="Camera Image - Nike"/></td><td><img src="small-005772L_20171211_5_1_1_csafe_kruse-bryson-zwart.jpg" alt="Film Image - Nike"/></td><td><img src="small-005772L_20180411_6_1_1_kruse_zwart_Kruse.jpg" alt="Paper Image - Nike"/></td></tr>
<tr><td><img src="small-007961L_20171031_2_1_1_csafe_bpbryson.jpg"  alt="Digital Scan Image - Adidas"/></td><td><img src="small-007961L_20171030_4_1_1_csafe_hdzwart.jpg" alt="Camera Image - Adidas"/></td><td><img src="small-007961L_20171211_5_1_1_csafe_kruse-bryson-zwart.jpg" alt="Film Image - Adidas"/></td><td><img src="small-007961L_20180411_6_1_1_kruse_zwart_Kruse.jpg" alt="Paper Image - Adidas"/></td></tr></table>

<div class="move-margin">
<img src="small-005772L_20171108_7_1_1_csafe_bpbryson.jpg" alt = "Realistic crime scene print - Nike"/>
<img src="small-007961L_20171108_7_1_1_csafe_bpbryson.jpg" alt = "Realistic crime scene print - Adidas"/>
<img src="3d_shoes_Participant_005_Turntable_scan.png" alt="3D scan - high quality - Nike"/>
<img src="3d_shoes_Participant_007_Turntable_scan.png" alt="3D scan - high quality - Adidas"/>
</div>

???

The Center for Statistics and Applications in Forensic Evidence (or CSAFE, for short) was created 5 years ago through a grant from NIST. Before that point, there were relatively few people doing research in statistics and forensics. So when CSAFE started, everyone was pretty new to the field of forensics, even if we were well-trained statisticians. 

Thing is, in many cases, statisticians don't collect their own data. So hopefully, you wouldn't make these mistakes. I wasn't around for the design (or most of the execution) of this study, but I'm involved in the analysis and have seen the fallout. 

We started out by creating a giant study using new-to-us equipment for collecting data we'd never worked with before. Why not make this study serve multiple purposes, right? It'll be efficient that way.

We varied shoe size, shoe model, collected data on the level of use the shoes got, the height and weight of the participants, the whole deal. We monitored these participants for a semester to see how the wear patterns started to emerge. Sounds great, right?

What we didn't do is refine the data collection methods first. We didn't spend a lot of time consulting with experts in the field until the experiment was underway, only to find out we'd done things sub-optimally. We didn't know enough about the types of shoes to intentionally manipulate the materials or patterns in each of the two shoe models. 

You'll hear a lot about this experiment during this talk - I was working on this data when I got the invitation to give the talk, so you might say this study was my inspiration. In my opinion, there were a couple of large errors made in this study, and they can be summarized as "trying to do too much, too soon". Sometimes you have to do the study while you have the money, but we would have been much better off starting with several smaller studies and then doing the giant factorial experiment of doom.


---
class:primary
## Jump Right In: <br/>Milk Nutrition Study

[Lanarkshire Milk Experiment](https://www.jstor.org/stable/2332424) described by Student (of Student's T-test fame)

- 20,000 children spread across 67 schools

- 5,000 given pasteurized milk, 5,000 given raw milk, 10,000 controls

- Several issues:
  - Random assignment problems
  - Weighing children with clothes (winter vs. summer weight)
  - Combining controls for groups (preventing school-wise comparison)

???
This problem is incidentally, as old as formal studies (at least). I found a paper from William Gosset, who published under the name "A student" describing a similarly gigantic study with several methodological issues that severely limited the impact. The paper is actually very interesting. 

The main reason I bring this up is that over and over again, we see that starting out with a smaller study might be a better option for refining the experimental design and methodology.

--

***
.center[Starting out with smaller studies might have made these issues apparent]

---
class:primary
## Don't get in over your head too quickly
.center[

<img src="https://j.gifs.com/GvAD20.gif" alt="corgi jumping into snow gif" width = "80%"/>

]
---
class:inverse,center,middle
# \# 2. Pilot studies are a waste of time

---
class:primary
## Pilot studies are a waste <br/>of time

Statisticians: 
- pilot studies are to get error estimates (classroom context)

- determine power/study size for the main study

If you don't really need error estimates, and you design your main study to be as big as is feasible, you don't need a pilot study, right?

--

.center[
<img src="https://media.giphy.com/media/lhfnAjmZQHEpa/giphy.gif" alt="Picard facepalm gif" width = "50%"/>
]


---
class:primary
## Pilot studies

- Pilot studies give you a chance to test
    - data collection methods
    - equipment
    - lab assistants
    - data cleaning/processing pipeline
    - statistical analysis methods/code
    
- You can write 80% of your paper before you start the main study if you have pilot data + Rmarkdown/knitr/Sweave
    - Just plug new data in and re-generate your results

- If everything goes perfectly during the pilot study and you don't change anything afterwards, you can usually throw the data into the main study (e.g. Phase 1 and Phase 2)
--

> (I've never seen this happen)

---
class:primary
## Pilot-ish studies

Sometimes, you can't easily get the pilot data you need.

In *most* cases, you can still construct a dataset that will let you test your methods, pipeline, etc., if not your exact hypothesis.

.move-margin[
<img src="new-balance-fresh-foam-zante-v4-ice-blue-pigment_product_8990306_color_764843.jpg" alt="New Balance sneaker"/>
<img src="converse-kids-chuck-taylor-all-star-core-hi-little-kid-black_product_7553444_color_3.png" alt="Converse sneaker"/>
<img src="el-naturalista-nido-n787-caldera_product_8965768_color_24838.png" alt="El Naturalista shoe"/>
]

<table class="blank"><tr><td><img src="Mantis3.png" alt="Shoe Scanner Model"/></td><td><img src="20190328_152239.jpg" alt="Shoe Scanner Prototype"/></td><td><img src="GOPR1219-blur-enhance.JPG" alt="Picture from prototype"</td></tr></table>

---
class:inverse
# \# 3. Data collection for the sake of data collection

---
class:primary
## Data collection<br/>for its own sake

Very context dependent.

- Every study should have a goal in mind

- Sometimes, that goal can be stated as a hypothesis

- It's generally not worth collecting the data unless you have a purpose for it.

.center[
<img src="https://media.giphy.com/media/3VydkbG6UOcUg/giphy.gif" alt="Pointless gif" width="60%"/>
]
???
I should start out by asking... do any of you have someone leading your group, your organization, or your section who is enthusiastic to a fault, but tends to not want to get into the details?

CSAFE is about to launch a project named "Project Tread" which is supposed to be primarily an outreach project. With that said, we're statisticians, and we can't completely write off the idea of using the data for ... something... at some point in time.

I'll confess that I'm a data packrat. I have datasets sitting on my machine that are obscure and probably useless, but I can't bring myself to delete them. However, not thinking about what you want to do with the data can lead to some really big problems down the road.

---
class:primary
## Data collection<br/>for its own sake
Exploratory data collection still involves goals!

**Example: Shoe data**

  - Want lots of shoe prints
  - Need to be able to resolve patterns on the shoe
  - Need to have a good scale for the size of the shoe
  - Ideally would be able to resolve randomly acquired damage (1mm or smaller)
  - Some of the prints should be smudged, just like in a crime scene
  - Want photo of the bottom of the shoe as well

***

  - Care less about variability in camera pictures
  - Don't need repetitions of the same print
  - Probably don't need both left and right shoe
  

.move-margin[
![Resulting print](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/CSAFE-ISU/this-is-us/master/images/shoes/ProjectTread/SP2_1.PNG)
]

--
***
These goals inform data collection methods and the design of the study

???

So using this particular project as an example, we forced ourselves to come up with the following uses:

Ideally, we'd still have a specific analysis we would want to do with the collected data. Maybe even some code for cleaning that data. But, even with the really rough goals we have listed above, we changed the protocol of the experiment to make the downstream data processing easier with one simple modification: we're now asking each person to trace the bottom of their shoe and upload that picture with all of the shoe print pictures. That image will serve as a "mask" and help us clean up the other images.

---
class:inverse
# \# 4. You can figure out how to analyze the data after it's collected

---
class:primary
## You can figure out how to analyze the data later
![inside out angry gif](https://media1.tenor.com/images/54d526fd183bb842780b9df05ebf6af6/tenor.gif?itemid=5628546)

---
class:primary
## You can figure out how to analyze the data later

**Statistics**: you should set up your hypotheses and analysis methods before you collect the data.

**Data Science**: 

1. You need to test the cleaning pipeline before you collect all of the data
.center[
<img alt="scrubbing pup gif" src="https://media.giphy.com/media/7JC6PAr1eyX8bBwhgV/giphy.gif" width="50%"/>
]

---
class:primary
## You can figure out how to analyze the data later

**Statistics**: you should set up your hypotheses and analysis methods before you collect the data.

**Data Science**: 

1. You need to know how to clean the data before you can analyze it
2. How you want to analyze the data may change the methods you use to collect it

.center[
<img alt="experiment gif" src="https://media.giphy.com/media/7MZ0v9KynmiSA/giphy.gif" width="65%"/>
]
---
class:primary
## You can figure out how to analyze the data later

**Statistics**: you should set up your hypotheses and analysis methods before you collect the data.

**Data Science**: 

1. You need to know how to clean the data before you can analyze it
2. How you want to analyze the data may change the methods you use to collect it
3. Developing the analysis pipeline early gives you a chance to find bugs 
<!-- (saving time, money, and retracted publications) -->


.center[
<img alt="analysis gif" src="https://media.giphy.com/media/13HgwGsXF0aiGY/giphy.gif" width="55%"/>
]
---
class:primary
## You can figure out how to analyze the data later

**Statistics**: you should set up your hypotheses and analysis methods before you collect the data.

**Data Science**: 

1. You need to know how to clean the data before you can analyze it
2. How you want to analyze the data may change the methods you use to collect it
3. Developing the analysis pipeline early gives you a chance to find bugs 


If you do the pilot study and analysis... you're already in the clear on this one

---
class:inverse
# \# 5. You don't need to know how your equipment works

---
class:primary
## Know your equipment

- Play with it (before you start data collection)
- Learn the different settings available
- Read the manual!
- Maintenance/calibration schedules

Handheld scan | Turntable scan
------------- | --------------
![Handheld scan image](3d_shoes_Participant_005_Handheld_scan.png) | ![Turntablel scan image](3d_shoes_Participant_005_Turntable_scan.png)


---
class:inverse
# \# 6. Allow your collaborators to change experimental procedures any time

---
class:primary
## Change experimental procedures

<table class="blank"><tr><td><img src="075718L_20171117_4_1_1_csafe_boekhoff.jpg" alt="Nov 2017"/></td><td><img src="075718L_20180207_4_1_1_csafe_hanrahan.jpg" alt="Feb 2018"/></td></tr><tr><td><img src="075718L_20180321_4_2_1_csafe_boekhoff.jpg" alt="Mar 2018"/></td><td><img src="075718L_20180509_4_1_1_csafe_jekruse.jpg" alt="May 20178"/></td></tr></table>
---
class:primary
## Change experimental procedures

Sometimes unavoidable changes
- Supplier ran out of XYZ, order from somewhere else
- Equipment broke and was replaced with new version
- Unavoidable personnel changes

<!-- .move-margin[![Exploding computer](https://media.giphy.com/media/nv99yd56AMNDa/giphy.gif)] -->

Unnecessary mid-experiment changes
- Black fingerprint powder will work better than silver
- Add another 3 rulers to the image 
- Change the order of data collection in a way that affects the data

---
class:inverse
# \# 7. Store your data in a proprietary or closed format

---
class:primary
## Proprietary Formats

- Depend on a company to maintain software to access the data

- Data isn't "future proof"

- Data can't be easily combined with other information

- Proprietary formats are less likely to be well documented

- Closed data formats are harder to get accepted to long-term data repositories

.center[Even the .doc format cannot be fully reverse engineered. ]

---
class:inverse
# \# 8. Don't make anything reproducible

---
class:primary
## Reproducibility

- Software such as knitr and Rmarkdown allow you to package your code, writing, and results in a single document that depends on your data

- Be kind to future you!

- Make your studies and data accessible to others

- Easily update and reuse analyses with new data

.move-down[
.move-margin[
![knitr hex sticker](https://www.libraries.rutgers.edu/sites/default/files/styles/resize_to_300px_width/public/events/2019/09/knitr.png)
![rmarkdown hex sticker](https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/images/hex-rmarkdown.png)
]]
---
class:inverse
# \# 9. Make everything reproducible, and then overwrite your data

---
class:inverse
# \# 10. No one is that interested in your data, so why share it?
